title: Bloody Sunday — Edmund Pettus Bridge
query: bloody sunday edmund pettus bridge selma march 1965 state troopers first-person account
blurb: The march from Selma toward Montgomery on March 7, 1965, halted at the foot of the Edmund Pettus Bridge by Alabama state troopers. Witnesses, organizers, and marchers describe what they saw.

passages.text_preview: The, the Committee had obtained the use of - vehicles to use as ambulances, a hearse or something like that. I was in one vehicle. There were a number of them. And we got to the [Edmund] Pettus Bridge, and the people were streaming back over the bridge. And, uh, the sheriff was standing there, and he wouldn't let me cr

passages.entry_subject: Geraldine Crawford Bennett, Toni Breaux, and Willie Elliot Jenkins
passages.text_preview: [Laughter] So, when people jumped the fence - well, they were jumping over the cars - Jumping on a razor. They was cutting them and throwing them - Just slicing them - Just slice them and then pass them on down. And one of the white men said - Have you ever heard that Klansman call us - Wait a minute. They had one whit

It was kind of funny. The SNCC office was directly across the street from the police station. And we could actually see people in jail through our window. So this is very physically close proximity. Very different from the image we get of the police in Selma because all we have is pictures of pushing back and beating.

I was walking by this little old lady and this state trooper came in between us. And he had his three-fifth of said, Magnum up gone. He pulled his trigger and said, Run, Nigger, pow, Ron Nigger. And this little old lady on this walking cane, right? And he finally reached over and knocked to walking cane out of the mud

passages.entry_subject: David Mercer Ackerman and Satoko Ito Ackerman
passages.text_preview: There were, uh, Alabama troopers with sunglasses, which were reflective glasses, which you could not see their faces, and they were wearing, you know, helmets. And then, they stood like this with the baton. And they looked so fierce! They looked so fierce, and they were everywhere. They were - all of a sudden, after we

Now, the state troopers were there to guard us, not protect us. And we spent the night in the park. But the next morning they went to search all the black people's cars. And that was the one time that I was proud as a Dicas Cossi. When we were on at March, we didn't have, people would let us use the Baton. We couldn't

And Tom Coleman, like a wild man, was flinging his gun and saying, I'll blow your brains out if we tried to give, if we didn't leave. He let that man lay down there in the hot, all the sun, shot begging for water and threatened to kill anybody who helped him. We dispersed and ran in different directions. And somehow I

I hadn't even thought up anything. In fact, I had no idea what was going to happen when I got there. I thought they would let us in and we would have some sort of encounter or conversation or whatever with whoever was in charge of the voting. The courthouse? Yeah, see what I mean?
